reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The opening guardrails-vs-alarms comparison table directly addresses the section instruction and is immediately useful for first-timers deciding whether they even need guardrails.
All 15 GuardrailType variants from the guardrails.d.ts union are accounted for — no type is silently omitted.
The four-step enforcement sequence (load config → fetch guardrails → validate → halt) gives first-timers a clear mental model before they see any guardrail fire in practice.
The 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' sections are both H2-level peers (correct hierarchy per style guide), substantive, and genuinely opinionated.

The FAQ hits all three required question categories: Stacktape-specific (who can manage guardrails), AWS-service (how guardrails differ from SCPs), and comparison/choosing (guardrails vs nothing for small teams).
The Warning callout about org-wide scope with no per-project override surfaces a critical gotcha — first-timers frequently assume per-environment controls exist.
No mandatory style violations: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript wrapping defineConfig.
problems: Specific database engine strings listed under 'Allowed database engines' (postgres, mysql, mariadb, aurora-postgresql, aurora-mysql, aurora-postgresql-serverless, etc.) are not enumerated in guardrails.d.ts — allowedEngines is typed as string[] with no explicit values. Listing 14 specific engine names is an unsupported Stacktape-specific claim.

Function memory range (128–10,240 MB) and timeout range (1–900 seconds) are not present in guardrails.d.ts — those numbers come from Lambda service limits, not from guardrail type definitions. FunctionMemoryLimitGuardrail only exposes maxMemoryMB?: number with no bounds.

The require-vpc-databases description claims it covers 'relational databases and OpenSearch domains' but the source JSDoc reads only 'all databases must use VPC-only accessibility' — OpenSearch is not mentioned.
function-memory-limit and function-timeout-limit are described as applying to 'Lambda functions and edge functions' but the source JSDoc for both types says only 'Lambda functions.'

Command restriction lists specific command names (deploy, delete, codebuild:deploy, rollback, cf:rollback, dev, preview-changes) — blockedCommands is typed as string[] in guardrails.d.ts with no enum. These names are unverified against the provided source.

resource-type-restriction is placed under 'Database restrictions' but its type name and JSDoc ('Block entire resource types from being used') indicate a general, not database-specific, guardrail. Grouping it there could mislead readers into thinking it only applies to database resource types.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the enumerated database engine strings from the 'Allowed database engines' bullet — guardrails.d.ts defines allowedEngines as string[] with no explicit values. Replace with: 'Configure which database engine types are permitted. The available values match the engine identifiers used by Stacktape relational databases — see [Relational databases](/resources/databases/relational-database) for supported engines.'

Remove the numeric ranges '128–10,240 MB' for memory and '1–900 seconds' for timeout from the resource limits section. guardrails.d.ts defines maxMemoryMB?: number and maxTimeoutSeconds?: number with no bounds specified. State only: 'Set a maximum memory allocation (in MB)' and 'Set a maximum timeout (in seconds)' without inventing range constraints.

Remove 'and OpenSearch domains' from the require-vpc-databases description. The source JSDoc says 'all databases must use VPC-only accessibility' only — OpenSearch is not named in the provided source for this guardrail. Use the source-grounded wording.

Remove 'and edge functions' from both the function-memory-limit and function-timeout-limit descriptions. The source JSDoc for FunctionMemoryLimitGuardrail and FunctionTimeoutLimitGuardrail reads 'Lambda functions' only — edge function coverage is ungrounded in the provided source.
optionalImprovements: Relocate resource-type-restriction out of 'Database restrictions' — its own category (e.g., 'Resource restrictions') or a note under 'Deployment restrictions' would better reflect its general scope and prevent readers from thinking it is database-only.

For the command restriction bullet, instead of listing specific command names (which aren't enumerated in the source type), link to the CLI reference index and say 'any Stacktape CLI command' — this is both more accurate and more durable as commands change.

The 'Managing guardrails' section describes console navigation steps (navigate → toggle → expand → save). This is grounded in the ToggleGuardrailRow/ValueGuardrailRow structure visible in GuardrailsPage.tsx, so it's acceptable, but a note that the exact UI may evolve would reduce future maintenance burden.

Consider adding a one-line example of what a GUARDRAIL error message looks like (e.g., 'Guardrail violated: require-vpc-databases — relational database "mainDb" has accessibility: public') so first-timers know what to expect when a deployment fails. This is high-value for the FAQ answer about what happens on violation.

The Tip callout about Aurora Serverless engines not being checked for instance sizes is accurate in spirit but cites unsupported source detail — guardrails.d.ts doesn't describe this exemption. Either find grounding or soften to a caveat ('check the database detail page').

Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Clear guardrails-vs-alarms distinction table at the top — immediately actionable for engineers deciding where to enforce policy.

Strong 'When NOT to use' section with concrete scenarios (solo teams, experimentation stages, all-or-nothing enforcement gotcha).

problems: The function-memory-limit section states 'range 128–10,240' MB and the function-timeout-limit section states 'range 1–900' seconds. Neither range appears in the guardrail source file — the types define only `maxMemoryMB?: number` and `maxTimeoutSeconds?: number` with no documented guardrail-level constraint. These are Lambda runtime limits, not guardrail constraints. Stating them as guardrail ranges could cause engineers to set values outside whatever the actual validation logic accepts.

The function memory limit and function timeout limit descriptions include 'edge functions' as in-scope targets. The guardrail source (FunctionMemoryLimitGuardrail, FunctionTimeoutLimitGuardrail) JSDoc says only 'Lambda functions'. Edge function coverage is unverified from the provided source.

The `resource-type-restriction` guardrail is placed under the 'Database restrictions' category. Its type definition (`blockedResourceTypes?: StpResourceType[]`) covers any Stacktape resource type — not just databases. The usage examples in the doc (open-search-domain, redis-cluster) are non-database types. This miscategorization will confuse engineers looking for general resource blocking.

The 'Database restrictions' section enumerates specific allowed engine strings (postgres, mysql, mariadb, aurora-postgresql, aurora-mysql, aurora-postgresql-serverless, aurora-mysql-serverless, aurora-postgresql-serverless-v2, aurora-mysql-serverless-v2, oracle-ee, oracle-se2, sqlserver-ee, sqlserver-ex, sqlserver-se, sqlserver-web). The guardrail source defines only `allowedEngines?: string[]` with no enumeration. Publishing this list as the supported set implies it is exhaustive, which cannot be confirmed from the provided source.

The 'require-vpc-databases' guardrail source JSDoc says 'all databases must use VPC-only accessibility.' The doc narrows this to 'relational databases and OpenSearch domains.' The narrowed scope is not grounded in the guardrail source.

The claim that only Owner and Admin roles can manage guardrails is stated as fact but does not appear in either provided source file (GuardrailsPage.tsx or guardrails.d.ts). No RBAC logic is visible in the Console source.

The Tip callout ('Aurora Serverless engines (v1 and v2) are not checked for instance sizes since they auto-scale') is not in the guardrail source file. This is an asserted behavioral claim without grounding.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the parenthetical ranges '(in MB, range 128–10,240)' and '(in seconds, range 1–900)' from the resource limits section. These ranges are not in the guardrail source (FunctionMemoryLimitGuardrail.maxMemoryMB and FunctionTimeoutLimitGuardrail.maxTimeoutSeconds are untyped numbers). If you want to help engineers understand valid values, link to the Lambda function docs instead.

Remove 'edge functions' from the function memory limit and function timeout limit descriptions. The guardrail source JSDoc scopes both to 'Lambda functions' only. Replace with 'Lambda functions' until edge function coverage is confirmed.

Move `resource-type-restriction` out of the 'Database restrictions' section. It targets `StpResourceType[]` (any resource type) — not just database types. Either add it to 'Deployment restrictions' alongside stage/region/command restriction, or create a standalone 'Resource restrictions' category.

Remove the specific database engine name list from the 'Database restrictions' section. The `database-engine-restriction` source defines only `allowedEngines?: string[]` with no enumeration in the provided source. Write instead: 'Specify which engine type strings are permitted; others are blocked' and link to the relational-database page for engine options.

Replace or remove the unsupported specificity in the require-vpc-databases description. The source says 'all databases' — not 'relational databases and OpenSearch domains.' Write 'all databases' or narrow only if another source confirms the scope.
Either remove the Owner/Admin role claim entirely or add an explicit hedge ('consult your organization settings') since this is not grounded in either provided source file.
optionalImprovements: The Tip about Aurora Serverless not being checked for instance sizes is a useful callout but is ungrounded — either find a source or remove it.

The enforcement-sequence numbered list is excellent; consider adding a one-line note about what happens to in-flight deployments (i.e., whether a guardrail toggled during an active deploy applies immediately or on the next run) if that behavior is documented elsewhere.

The FAQ answer for 'Do guardrails apply to dev mode?' says resource-level guardrails 'are evaluated against the Stacktape configuration regardless of which command is running' — this is a strong claim not visible in the provided source. Soften or source it.

Consider adding a brief note that there is no API or CLI for managing guardrails — the Console is the only management surface. This is practically useful context for teams running fully automated pipelines.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and accurately covers all 15 guardrail types from the source. The main factual issues concern enforcement scope: the draft overstates which CLI commands evaluate guardrails. Only deploy, codebuild-deploy, delete, and preview-changes call validateGuardrails — but the draft claims dev mode is subject to resource-level guardrails and implies all deployment paths are covered. The command-restriction list of 7 blockable commands is correct per the Console UI, but only 4 of those commands actually enforce the block at runtime. The resource-type-restriction guardrail is miscategorized under 'Database restrictions' when it can block any resource type. Minor issues include Lambda platform ranges presented as guardrail constraints and an unsourced subscription plan claim.

The draft is directionally aligned with the guardrail type definitions, but it makes several strong Stacktape-specific behavior, Console, permission, lifecycle, and pricing claims that are not supported by the provided sources. The most important fixes are to narrow deployment-enforcement details to what `guardrails.d.ts` actually states, remove invented Console/role workflow details, and avoid claiming plan/cost behavior without billing source evidence.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is self-contained and accurately frames guardrails as preventive (deploy-time) vs alarms as reactive (runtime) — a distinction that anchors the entire page and is directly extractable by AI engines.

All 15 guardrail types from the source union type are accurately covered and mapped to their correct property names (allowedStages, allowedRegions, blockedCommands, maxMemoryMB, blockedInstanceSizes, etc.).

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph (under 75 words) is self-contained, fact-dense, and directly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — ideal for AI Overview extraction.

The GuardrailType JSDoc in guardrails.d.ts reads only 'all databases must use VPC-only accessibility (no public internet access)' — no mention of OpenSearch and no mode names. reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is 44 words, fully self-contained, and directly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' — ideal for AI extraction.
Guardrails vs alarms comparison table is the clearest possible way to separate two easily-confused concepts. Every column is concrete (When, Action, Scope, Purpose, Managed in).
All 15 guardrail types from guardrails.d.ts are covered and accurately described, including the correct property names (allowedStages, blockedCommands, maxMemoryMB, maxCpu, etc.).
Info callout that clarifies the resource-type restriction sitting under 'Database restrictions' category is a genuine user-trap and the callout handles it well.
'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' correctly appear as peer H2 sections rather than nesting 'When NOT to use' under 'When to use'.

FAQ contains exactly 10 questions covering all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (can I block stage deletion?), AWS-service use-case (how do guardrails differ from SCPs?), and choosing/comparison (guardrails vs IAM).

No forbidden patterns detected: no YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, no marketing language.
Section headings are compact noun-form labels (Deployment restrictions, Security and data protection, Resource limits, Database restrictions) — consistent and scannable.
problems: The 'Resource limits' section states the function-memory and function-timeout guardrails apply to 'Lambda functions and edge functions', but the guardrails.d.ts JSDoc for FunctionMemoryLimitGuardrail and FunctionTimeoutLimitGuardrail only says 'Lambda functions'. Claiming edge functions are covered is not supported by the provided source.

The container-resource-limit description enumerates specific resource types (web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads) but the source type ContainerResourceLimitGuardrail only says 'container workloads' without enumerating specific sub-types. The enumeration is plausible but not grounded in this page's source files.

The 'Managing guardrails' section describes navigational UI detail (clicking expand, clicking Save, clicking Remove) which is source-grounded here, but the section says guardrails are managed at the 'selected organization' level without noting what role or permission level is required to manage them — a question real users will have.

No ApiReference component anywhere on the page. For a concept/governance page, the GuardrailDefinition union type exists in guardrails.d.ts and likely has a schema entry. Its absence is not a hard rule violation for an overview page, but the pipeline does expect it on concept pages that have a root type.
optionalImprovements: Qualify the edge function claim: the JSDoc for FunctionMemoryLimitGuardrail and FunctionTimeoutLimitGuardrail only says 'Lambda functions'. Either verify that EdgeLambdaFunction is covered by these guardrails (and cite the evidence) or remove 'and edge functions' from both descriptions to stay within what the source supports.

Replace 'container workloads, including web services, private services, worker services, and multi-container workloads' with just 'container workloads' in the container-resource-limit description, since the source type only says 'container workloads' without enumerating sub-types.

Add a one-sentence access note to 'Managing guardrails': who can manage organization-level guardrails (admin, owner)? The Console source shows an org-scoped mutation but doesn't gate by role on this page — link to the team and access control page as a reference rather than inventing a role name.

Consider adding an <ApiReference definitionName='GuardrailDefinition' /> (or the equivalent schema entry name) somewhere on the page, either at the bottom or after the 'How guardrails work' section, so readers can drill into individual guardrail type shapes without leaving the page.

The 'Database restrictions' section uses 'Blocked instance sizes' in the heading but 'Blocked instance sizes' as the guardrail name, while the type is database-instance-restriction. Consider adding a one-word note that the underlying type identifier is 'database-instance-restriction' to help readers who search logs or error messages for the guardrail name.

The FAQ answer for 'Do guardrails apply to already-deployed stacks?' is accurate but could be sharpened: stacks that were deployed before a guardrail was activated are NOT retroactively blocked from running, only from future operations. The current wording says this implicitly but a direct 'already-running resources are not affected' sentence would make it clearer for readers who are worried about breaking live infrastructure.

The FAQ answer for 'Can I set different guardrails for production vs development stages?' correctly says guardrails are per-organization, but the recommendation to use separate organizations is not elaborated. Add one sentence noting the practical tradeoff: separate organizations means separate billing, access management, and provider configuration, so this is only warranted for genuinely different policy regimes.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-grounded with accurate guardrail type enumeration, property names, and category groupings verified against both guardrails.d.ts and guardrails-schema.tsx. Three substantive issues: (1) the require-vpc-databases guardrail omits OpenSearch domains which the Console schema explicitly includes (high), (2) function memory/timeout limits are extended to edge functions without source support (medium), and (3) multi-container workloads are added to the container resource limit scope beyond what the source lists (medium). Two minor ambiguities around enforcement timing and badge display behavior round out the findings.

The draft is mostly aligned with the guardrail type definitions, but it makes several unsupported Stacktape-specific behavior and Console workflow claims that are not grounded in the provided sources. The biggest risks are claims about enforcement timing, error messages, bypass behavior, category organization, and already-deployed stacks.

issues.statement: “When a guardrail check fails, the operation fails with a clear error message identifying which guardrail was violated — no infrastructure changes are made.” Similar claims appear in the FAQ: “caught before any AWS API calls run” and “No infrastructure changes are made.”
issues.reason: The provided guardrails type file defines guardrail shapes and properties, but it does not document enforcement timing, transactionality, AWS API call ordering, or error-message contents. This is a Stacktape-specific behavior claim that would affect reader expectations.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow to the sourced behavior: guardrails define organization-level restrictions such as allowed stages, allowed regions, blocked commands, and resource limits. Avoid claiming exact failure timing, AWS API call ordering, or error-message contents unless runtime validator/CLI source is provided.
